43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	\begin{lemma}
185	
186	\leanok
187	  [Smooth \(\Rightarrow\) DVR at every codim-\(1\) point]
188	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
190	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
191	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
192	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
193	  % PDF page 147).
194	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in
195	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
196	  % local ring \(\mathcal O_x\) of \(X\) of dimension one is regular."
197	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
198	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
199	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the
200	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of
201	  % closed points."
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
203	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
204	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\)."
205	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
206	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
207	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
208	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
209	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).
210	\end{lemma}
211	
212	\begin{proof}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  By smoothness, \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular for every closed point \(x \in X\)
216	  (Hartshorne I.5.1). Localising a regular local ring at a prime ideal yields a regular
217	  local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
218	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular
219	  of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
220	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
221	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
222	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
223	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
224	
225	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
226	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} is the
227	  instance/lemma stating that \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a Mathlib
228	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
229	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
230	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta\) = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
231	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance
232	  \texttt{Scheme.fractionRing\_localRing\_eq\_functionField} (or whatever Mathlib name
233	  the prover chooses at commit \texttt{b80f227}). The regular-local-ring-of-dimension-1
234	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) DVR equivalence is shipped in Mathlib as
235	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} (or analogous);
236	  the prover re-exports it rather than re-proving.
237	\end{proof}
238	
239	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
240	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
241	
242	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
243	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
244	point.
245	
246	\begin{theorem}
247	
248	\leanok
249	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
250	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
252	  
253	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
254	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
255	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
256	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
257	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
258	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
259	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
260	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
261	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
262	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
263	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
264	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
265	
266	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
267	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
268	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
269	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
270	  in \(X\).
271	\end{theorem}
272	
273	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
274	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
275	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
276	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
277	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
278	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
279	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
280	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
281	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
282	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
283	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
284	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
285	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
286	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
287	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
288	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
289	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
290	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
291	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
292	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
293	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
294	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
295	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
296	% which is a contradiction."
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
300	
301	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
302	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
303	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
304	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
305	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
306	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
307	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
308	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
309	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
310	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
311	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
312	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
313	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
314	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
315	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
316	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
317	
318	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
319	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
320	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
321	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
322	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
323	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
324	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
325	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
326	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
327	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
328	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
329	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
330	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
331	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
332	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
333	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
334	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
335	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
336	
337	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
338	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
339	  agree on all of \(X\).
340	\end{proof}
341	
342	\begin{remark}
343	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
344	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
345	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
346	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
347	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
348	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
349	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
350	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
351	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
352	
353	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
354	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
355	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
356	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
357	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
358	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
359	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
360	\end{remark}
361	
362	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
363	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
364	
365	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
366	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
367	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
368	this forces the locus to be empty.
369	
370	\begin{lemma}
371	
372	\leanok
373	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
374	  variety]
375	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
377	  
378	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
379	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
380	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
381	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
382	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
383	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
384	  % divisors).}"
385	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
386	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
387	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
388	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
389	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
390	  union of prime divisors).
391	
392	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
393	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
394	\end{lemma}
395	
396	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
397	%   \(\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G\), $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
398	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
399	% More precisely, if \((U, \varphi_U)\) represents \(\varphi\), then \(\Phi\) is the rational
400	% map represented by
401	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
402	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
403	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
404	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at
405	% \(x\), and then \(\Phi(x, x) = e\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\), then
406	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an
407	% open subset \(U\) of \(V\) such that \(\Phi\) is defined on \(\{x\} \times U\). After
408	% possible replacing \(U\) by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing \(x\)),
409	% \(\varphi\) will be defined on \(U\). For \(u \in U\), the formula
410	%   \(\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)\)
411	% defines \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Thus \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) if and only if \(\Phi\) is
412	% defined at \((x, x)\). The rational map \(\Phi\) defines a map
413	%   \(\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)\).
414	% Since \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) if it is defined there, it follows that \(\Phi\)
415	% is defined at \((x, x)\) if and only if
416	%   \(\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}\).
417	% Now \(V \times V\) is nonsingular, and so we have a good theory of divisors (AG,
418	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function \(f\) on \(V \times V\), write
419	%   \(\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\),
420	% with \(\operatorname{div}(f)_0\) and \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\) effective divisors
421	% --- note that \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0\). Then
422	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
423	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
424	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
425	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
426	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
427	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
428	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
429	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
430	\begin{proof}
431	  
432	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
433	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
434	
435	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
436	  \[
437	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
438	    \qquad
439	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
440	  \]
441	  via the composition
442	  \[
443	    U \times U
444	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
445	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
446	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
447	  \]
448	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
449	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
450	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
451	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
452	
453	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
454	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
455	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
456	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
457	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
458	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
459	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
460	  identity
461	  \[
462	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
463	    \qquad u \in W',
464	  \]
465	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
466	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
467	
468	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
469	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
470	  \(K(X \times X)\):
471	  \[
472	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
473	  \]
474	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
475	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
476	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
477	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
478	
479	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
480	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
481	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
482	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
483	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
484	  \[
485	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
486	  \]
487	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
488	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
489	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
490	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
491	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
492	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
493	  \[
494	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
495	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
496	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
497	  \]
498	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
499	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
500	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
501	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
502	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
503	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
504	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
505	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
506	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
507	
508	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
509	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
510	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
511	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
512	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
513	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
514	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
515	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
516	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
517	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
518	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
519	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
520	\end{proof}
521	
522	\begin{remark}
523	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
524	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
525	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
526	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
527	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
528	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
529	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
530	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
531	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
532	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
533	\end{remark}

535	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
536	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
537	
538	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
539	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
540	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
541	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
542	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
543	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
544	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
545	
546	\begin{theorem}
547	
548	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
549	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
550	  
551	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):
552	  % `\lean{...}` pin DETACHED (Path D2 option (b) per the lvb-checker). The
553	  % substantive Hartshorne-II.6 valuative reformulation (the iff with
554	  % ord_W >= 0) below is the *intended* statement; the iter-179 Lane D
555	  % `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` decl is only a 2-LOC
556	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` reshuffle that does NOT prove the
557	  % ord_W content, so re-pinning `\lean{...}` to it would falsely claim
558	  % kernel-clean formalization of the substantive Hartshorne iff. The
559	  % weaker reshuffle proven this iter will be pinned to its own light
560	  % `\begin{lemma}` block (working title `lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle`)
561	  % by an iter-180+ blueprint-writer dispatch; the substantive Hartshorne
562	  % version stays pinned here but unlinked until a future
563	  % `extend_iff_pullback_order_nonneg` lemma carries
564	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.order` machinery (pullback `RationalMap →
565	  % function-field ring map`, not yet in Mathlib at b80f227). See
566	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-codimone-iter179.md +
567	  % proof-journal/sessions/session_179/recommendations.md REC-14.
568	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
569	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
570	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
571	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
573	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
574	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
575	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
576	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
577	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
578	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
579	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
580	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
581	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
582	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
583	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
584	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
585	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
586	  \begin{enumerate}
587	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
588	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
589	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
590	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
591	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
592	      satisfying
593	      \[
594	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
595	      \]
596	  \end{enumerate}
597	
598	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
599	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
600	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
601	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
602	\end{theorem}
603	
604	\begin{proof}
605	  
606	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
607	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
608	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
609	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
610	  \[
611	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
612	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
613	  \]
614	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
615	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
616	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
617	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
618	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
619	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
620	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
621	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
622	
623	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
624	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
625	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
626	\end{proof}
627	
628	\begin{remark}
629	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
630	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
631	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
632	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
633	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
634	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
635	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
636	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
637	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
638	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
639	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
640	\end{remark}

747	\section{Out of scope}
748	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
749	
750	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
751	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
752	scope:
753	\begin{itemize}
754	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
755	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
756	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
757	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
758	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
759	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
760	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
761	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
762	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
763	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
764	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
765	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
766	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
767	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
768	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
769	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
770	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
771	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
772	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
773	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
774	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
775	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
776	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
777	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
778	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
779	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
780	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
781	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
782	    introduced.
783	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
784	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
785	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
786	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
787	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
788	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
789	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
790	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
791	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
792	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
793	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-\(0\) curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	label{chap:avr_for_rr}
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/ChartIso.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
10	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
11	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
12	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
13	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
14	% keystone directly). The headline prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
15	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
16	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
17	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
18	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
38	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
39	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
40	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
41	\mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\( (the M\"obius scaling fixing both \)0$ and
42	\(\infty\)), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
43	G_m\( (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising \)f(0) = \eta_A\( and composing \)h := \sigma_\times
44	\fatsemi f\(, one applies additivity of \)\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
45	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
46	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) proper, \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and
47	base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\). Because \(0\) is a \emph{fixed point} of
48	scaling, the \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)\) is
49	\emph{constant} at \(\eta_A\), so the decomposition collapses to \(h = p \fatsemi f\), i.e.\
50	\(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\). Specialising \(x = 1\) gives that \(f|_{\mathbb G_m}\) is constant, and since
51	\(\mathbb G_m\) is dense in \(\mathbb P^1\) with \(A\) separated, \(f\) is constant
52	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
53	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
54	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
55	\(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\), and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
56	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive
57	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility
58	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
59	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-\(0\) critical path. The corollary that a
60	pointed regular map of abelian
61	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
62	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-\(0\) path; Milne's
63	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
64	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-\(0\) critical path
65	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
66	gives that every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant
67	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-\(0\)
68	curves (\cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}, \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) over \(\bar k\)) yields the
69	headline \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
70	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
71	neither Serre duality nor the FGA \(\Pic\) representability engine, so the genus-\(0\) arm is
72	fully decoupled from Route~A.
73	\medskip
74	Throughout, \(\bar k\) is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
75	\emph{abelian variety} over \(\bar k\) is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
76	project's Lean encoding \(A\) is an object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) carrying
77	\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), and \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\).
78	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
79	\begin{lemma}
80	\leanok
81	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
82	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
83	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
84	  
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
86	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
88	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
89	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
90	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
91	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
92	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
93	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety, and let \(Y\), \(Z\) be any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
94	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism such that for some \(y_0 \in Y\) the image
95	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0 \in Z\). Then there is a morphism
96	  \(g \colon Y \to Z\) with \(f = g \circ p_2\), where \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
97	  projection; that is, \(f\) depends only on the \(Y\)-coordinate, and \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) for any
98	  fixed \(x_0 \in X\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
101	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
102	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
103	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
104	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
105	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
106	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
107	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
108	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
109	% assertion."
110	\begin{proof}
111	\leanok

116	% The counterexample f = fst is excluded (under f = fst, _hf forces X to collapse to a point).
117	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open is sorry-free in its OWN body (hfib, the pullback-fibre fact over the

122	% lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open. No backward edge from the leaf lemmas back up the chain.

126	%     reduced Jacobson source into a separated target are equal" -- PROVEN axiom-clean (the
127	%     dense-closed-points coproduct probe + ext_of_isDominant; the connective Mathlib lacks).
128	%   - Step 1, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine: Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy
129	%     -- the genuinely-deep residual, still `sorry`. THIS is now the chain's lone deep residual

131	% SIGNATURE CHANGE (iter-161, plan-authorized): Step 2's globalisation needs the closed points of
132	% the saturated open U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson, which over Spec k-bar follows from (X tensor Y)

138	  Fix any point \(x_0 \in X\) and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) (the
139	  composite of \(y \mapsto (x_0, y)\) with \(f\)). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety (in
140	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
141	  the equality of morphisms \(f = g \circ p_2\) it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
142	  open subset of \(X \times Y\).
143	  Let \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U\) (a closed set),
144	  and set \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection
145	  \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
146	  completeness/properness); hence \(G\), the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\), is closed in
147	  \(Y\). The hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U\) gives
148	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), so \(y_0 \notin G\). Therefore
149	  \(V := Y - G\) is a non-empty open subset of \(Y\).
150	  For each \(y \in V\) we have \(X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing\), i.e.
151	  \(f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U\). Thus the complete (proper) variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is
152	  mapped by \(f\) into the \emph{affine} variety \(U\). A proper connected variety mapping to an
153	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
154	  variety is constant). Hence \(f(X \times \{y\})\) is one point, necessarily
155	  \(f(x_0, y) = g(y)\). So for all \(x \in X\) and \(y \in V\),
156	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
157	  The two morphisms \(f\) and \(g \circ p_2\) thus agree on the non-empty open
158	  \(X \times V\), which is dense in the irreducible \(X \times Y\); as \(Z\) is separated they
159	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
160	\end{proof}
161	\begin{remark}
162	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
163	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of \(X\) makes
164	  \(p_2\) a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
165	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
166	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
167	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
168	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
169	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring \(V\) complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically
170	  irreducible.)
171	\end{remark}
172	\begin{remark}
173	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_decomposition}

175	  the proof above: \texttt{rigidity\_snd\_lift} (the cartesian-monoidal algebra identifying the
176	  collapsed map \((x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\) followed by \(f\) with \(g \circ p_2\));
177	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} (the scheme-level gluing: two maps out of the geometrically irreducible
178	  reduced \(X \times Y\) into the separated \(Z\) that agree on a non-empty open subset agree
179	  everywhere, by separatedness); and \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open}
180	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}, the genuine geometry: the construction of the non-empty
181	  open \(X \times V\) and the slice-constancy of \(f\) on it). The collapse hypothesis
182	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\) must be threaded through \emph{all three} helpers, since it is
183	  what makes \(V\) non-empty; a decomposition that drops it from the lower helpers is unsound

216	\begin{lemma}
217	\leanok
218	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
219	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
221	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
223	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
224	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
225	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
226	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
227	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
228	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
229	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety and \(Y\), \(Z\) any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
230	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a point \(x_0 \in X\), and suppose given
231	  \(y_0 \in Y\), \(z_0 \in Z\) with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
232	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
233	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
234	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
235	  Then there is a non-empty open subset \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) on which \(f\) agrees, as a
236	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\), where
237	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
238	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)\( is the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
239	  \(U = X \times V\) with \(V := Y - G\), \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))\) for an affine open
240	  neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(z_0\), and on \(U\) one has \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)\).
241	  \medskip
242	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of \(V\) is exactly
243	  the assertion \(y_0 \notin G\), and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
244	  \subseteq U_0\( keeps the slice over \)y_0\( inside the affine \)U_0\(, away from \)F = Z - U_0$. A
245	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for \(X = Y = Z\) and
246	  \(f = p_1\) the first projection, \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0\)
247	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in \(\{x = x_0\}\), which has empty
248	  interior in the irreducible \(X \times Y\)). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
249	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
250	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
251	  \medskip
252	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
253	  source.} The prose already takes \(\bar k\) algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
254	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
255	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field \(\kappa(y)\) to be \(\bar k\) itself, so that a
256	  proper integral slice has global sections \(\Gamma = \bar k\) and maps to a single \(\bar k\)-point of
257	  the affine \(U_0\); this requires \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\). Second, the globalisation of the
258	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
259	  closed points of the saturated open \(U\) to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ \(U\) to be a Jacobson space.
260	  Over the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) this holds precisely because \(X \times Y\) is \emph{locally of
261	  finite type} over \(\bar k\) (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
262	  property of \(\Spec \bar k\), and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
263	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\). Accordingly the formalization carries
264	  \emph{both} \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and
265	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\) on this lemma (in addition to
266	  \([\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]\)), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
267	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
268	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
269	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
270	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
271	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
272	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) --- the base-field variable is literally named \(\mathtt{kbar}\) ---
273	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over \(\bar k\), so the
274	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
275	\end{lemma}
276	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
277	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
278	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
279	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
280	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
281	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
282	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
283	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
284	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
285	% assertion."
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \leanok
288	  Let \(U_0\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U_0\), and set
289	  \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
290	  \emph{closed} map, so \(G\) (the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\)) is closed in \(Y\). The
291	  collapse hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0\) gives
292	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), hence \(y_0 \notin G\); this is the only place
293	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for \(V := Y - G\) is non-empty precisely
294	  because \(y_0 \in V\). For each \(y \in V\) the proper variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is mapped by \(f\)
295	  into the affine \(U_0\), hence to a single point, necessarily \(f(x_0, y)\). So for all \(x \in X\),
296	  \(y \in V\), we have \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)\), i.e.\ \(f\) and
297	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on the non-empty open \(U := X \times V\). (In the Lean
298	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
299	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
300	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
301	  \medskip
302	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
303	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
304	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
305	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
306	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``\(X\) complete \(\Rightarrow p_2\) closed'' is
307	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
308	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on \(X\), base-changed across the canonical pullback square
309	  to make \(p_2\) universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
310	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites \((\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}\) to
311	  \(\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}\) exactly. This step is already
312	  proven.
313	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of \(V\)) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
314	  \(X \times \{y_0\}\) over the \(\bar k\)-rational point \(y_0\) is exactly the image of the slice
315	  section \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
316	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
317	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
318	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
319	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1\) and
320	  \(s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0\) come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
321	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
322	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
323	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
324	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]\).
325	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
326	  ``Proper connected slice into affine \(\Rightarrow\) single point, glued to a morphism equality on
327	  \(U\)'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
328	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) statement: the
329	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no \(H^0\) base change), and route~(c)
330	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
331	  stack is: for each closed point \(y \in V\) one has \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\) (alg-closed, finite type);
332	  the slice \(X_y\) is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
333	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over \(\bar k\)
334	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
335	  extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring
336	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so \(f\) and
337	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
338	  locally-of-finite-type \(U\) (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
339	  locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
340	  hypothesis \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)), so the per-slice
341	  agreement globalises to all of \(U\) via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
342	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
343	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
344	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
345	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
346	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
347	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
348	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
349	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
350	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
351	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
352	\end{proof}

354	\leanok

356	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
357	  
358	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
359	  % NOTE (iter-161): the iter-160 SIGNATURE GAP is RESOLVED. The Lean proof wires Step 2
360	  % (morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, PROVEN axiom-clean) over Step 1
361	  % (rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, the residual sorry). Step 2 needs the closed
362	  % points of U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson; over k̄ this follows from (X⊗Y) being locally of finite

366	  % reachable from this lemma is now Step 1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy). The hypothesis

368	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom]); the earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim

370	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
372	  % \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
373	  % any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
374	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
375	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
376	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
377	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be objects of
378	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) with \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced,
379	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over \(\bar k\) (the formalization carries
380	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\), which makes the saturated open \(U\)
381	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
382	  consumes), and \(Z\) separated. Let \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a \(\bar k\)-point
383	  \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
384	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\( for the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
385	  endomorphism. Suppose:
386	  \begin{itemize}
387	    \item \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) is a \(p_2\)-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
388	      for a set \(V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y\) (so \(U\) contains the whole fibre \(X_y\) over each of
389	      its points);
390	    \item \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) is an \emph{affine} open;
391	    \item \(f(U) \subseteq U_0\), i.e.\ \(f\) maps \(U\) into the affine \(U_0\).
392	  \end{itemize}
393	  Then \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on \(U\) as scheme morphisms:
394	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\).
395	\end{lemma}
396	\begin{proof}
397	  \leanok
398	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete
399	  variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single
400	  point of \(U\)''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
401	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
402	  \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
403	  \(H^0\) base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
404	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.
405	  \medskip
406	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
407	  Fix a closed point \(x\) of \(U\), lying over a closed point \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\).
408	  Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type over \(\bar k\), the residue field
409	  is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Because \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) is saturated, \(U\) contains the
410	  \emph{entire} fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\); hence \(f\) maps the proper integral slice \(X_y\) into the
411	  affine \(U_0\). By \(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\) the global sections \(\Gamma(X_y)\) form a
412	  field, module-finite over \(\bar k\) by \(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\), and
413	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into
414	  the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so the
415	  restriction of \(f\) to \(X_y\) factors through a single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\) --- necessarily the
416	  point \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\). Thus \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\)
417	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of \(U\). This per-slice statement is the
418	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
419	  \medskip
420	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
421	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-scheme \(U\)
422	  (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\) / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
423	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)). To turn ``\(f\) and
424	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on \(U\),
425	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
426	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
427	  \(\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U\), whose range is dense --- and feeds
428	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (the same
429	  family \(\mathtt{rigidity\_core}\) uses). This yields the morphism equality
430	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\) on all of \(U\). The
431	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
432	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
433	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\)); that bespoke build is now done in
434	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
435	\end{proof}
436	\begin{lemma}\leanok
437	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
438	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
439
