441	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
442	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
443	  Let \(W\), \(Z\) be schemes with \(W\) \emph{reduced}, \(W\) a \emph{Jacobson space}, and \(Z\)
444	  \emph{separated}. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z\) be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
445	  \(x \in W\), the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
446	  \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to W\) (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
447	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\(), then \)g_1 = g_2$.
448	\end{lemma}
449	\begin{proof}
450	\leanok
451	  This is the ``dense closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
452	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
453	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
454	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
455	  dominant probe.
456	  Form the coproduct \(P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)\) and the morphism
457	  \(\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W\) defined by \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\) of the residue-field points
458	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\). Its topological range contains every closed point of \(W\):
459	  the inclusion \(\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P\) composes with \(\mathrm{probe}\) to
460	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) (the defining property of \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\)), and the
461	  range of \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) is the singleton \(\{x\}\)
462	  (\(\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}\)). Because \(W\) is a Jacobson space, the closed points
463	  are dense (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\), i.e.\ \(\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W\)), so
464	  the range of \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dense and \(\mathrm{probe}\) is \emph{dominant}.
465	  Componentwise, \(g_1\) and \(g_2\) agree after \(\mathrm{probe}\): precomposing with each coproduct
466	  inclusion \(\iota_x\) reduces (by \(\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}\)) to
467	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\),
468	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point \(x\). By the universal property of the coproduct
469	  (\(\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}\)) this gives \(\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2\).
470	  Since \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dominant and \(Z\) is separated, \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) cancels it
471	  on the left to yield \(g_1 = g_2\).
472	\end{proof}
473	\begin{lemma}
474	\leanok
475	[Global-sections constancy: two \(\bar k\)-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
476	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
478	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
479	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
480	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
481	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
482	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(W\) be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
483	  morphism \(w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k\) that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
484	  type. Let \(g \colon W \to V\) be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme \(V\). Then any two
485	  \(\bar k\)-points of \(W\) --- i.e.\ any two sections \(a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W\) of \(w\) (so
486	  \(a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\) and \(b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\)) --- satisfy \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\).
487	  \medskip
488	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
489	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) collapses the finite extension \(\Gamma(W)/\bar k\); integrality of
490	  \(W\) makes \(\Gamma(W)\) a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
491	  \(\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}\) is what upgrades \(\Gamma(W)\) to a \emph{field} (without it
492	  \(\mathbb A^1\), with \(\Gamma = \bar k[t]\) and two distinct \(\bar k\)-points, is a counterexample);
493	  \(\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\) gives finiteness of \(\Gamma(W)\) over \(\bar k\); and
494	  \([\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]\) is what lets \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\) pin the morphism by its ring map
495	  on global sections.
496	\end{lemma}
497	\begin{proof}
498	\leanok
499	  Because \(W\) is integral and \(w\) is universally closed, the global sections
500	  \(\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)\) form a field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), and the structure
501	  ring map \(w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)\) (the map \(\mathtt{wk.appTop}\) after the canonical
502	  identification \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k\)) is integral / module-finite
503	  (\(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\)). Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and
504	  \(\Gamma(W)\) is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
505	  (\(\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}\)); thus \(\Gamma(W) = \bar k\) and
506	  \(w^\sharp\) is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
507	  \(a\), \(b\) left-invert \(w^\sharp\) on global sections (they are sections of \(w\)), so they induce the
508	  \emph{same} ring map \(a^\sharp = b^\sharp\) on \(\Gamma(W)\) (cancel the epimorphism \(w^\sharp\)). A
509	  morphism into the \emph{affine} \(V\) is pinned by its global-sections ring map
510	  (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), whence \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\). This realises, without
511	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
512	  quoted from Mumford above.
513	\end{proof}
514	\begin{lemma}
515	\leanok
516	[Integrality descends to a retract of an integral scheme]
517	  \label{lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract}
519	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) project-bespoke (elementary topology + algebra); NO external source.
520	  % LANDED axiom-clean as `AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract`, stated generically for any
521	  % retract `r : S ⟶ T`, `pr : T ⟶ S` with `r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S` of an integral `T` (more general than
522	  % the `X`/`X×Y`/`p_1` instance below). PROOF DIVERGENCE (cosmetic, sound either way): the Lean
523	  % proves reducedness PER-STALK (each `S.stalk x` embeds via `pr.stalkMap` into the reduced
524	  % `T.stalk`, then `isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk`), not via the global-sections split-injection
525	  % the prose below describes. Plan/blueprint-writer may align the prose to the stalk route.
526	  Let \(X \times Y\) be an integral scheme (here it is geometrically irreducible and reduced over
527	  \(\bar k\)). Suppose \(X\) is a \emph{retract} of \(X \times Y\): there is a section
528	  \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) of the first projection \(p_1\), i.e.\ \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\).
529	  Then \(X\) is integral.
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	\leanok
533	  Integrality is reducedness together with irreducibility, and the retract supplies each.
534	  \emph{Reduced.} The section identity \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\) makes the pullback on global
535	  sections \(p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X(X) \to \mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) split
536	  injective (it is left-inverted by \(s^\sharp\)). A split-injective ring map into the reduced ring
537	  \(\mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) embeds \(\mathcal O_X(X)\) into a reduced ring, so it too is
538	  reduced (\(\mathtt{isReduced\_of\_injective}\) on the split injection); hence \(X\) is reduced.
539	  \emph{Irreducible.} The first projection \(p_1 \colon X \times Y \to X\) is surjective because it
540	  admits the section \(s\) (every point of \(X\) is \(p_1(s(\cdot))\)). The continuous image of an
541	  irreducible space under a surjection is irreducible; as \(X \times Y\) is irreducible and \(p_1\) is a
542	  continuous surjection onto \(X\), the space of \(X\) is irreducible.
543	  Reduced and irreducible together give that \(X\) is integral. This is elementary --- no cohomology,
544	  no external citation --- and feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly below, where the proper slice
545	  \(X_y \cong X\) must be presented as proper \emph{integral}.
546	\end{proof}

593	\section{The Milne \S I.3 chain: additivity, homomorphisms, extension}
594	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-\(0\) base case. The two
595	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
596	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
597	G_m\(-scaling action \)\sigma_\times\( on \)\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
598	the base case ``every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
599	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point \(0\) collapses one axis of the
600	decomposition, giving \(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\) and hence constancy on the dense \(\mathbb G_m\). The
601	classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive homomorphism step
602	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\)
603	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
604	\emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
605	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
606	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\( is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete \)\mathbb
607	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
608	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
609	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
610	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-\(0\)
611	path. The genus-\(0\) output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
612	(here: regular) map \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant''.
613	\begin{remark}
614	  \label{rmk:cube_not_needed}
615	  \textbf{The theorem of the cube is not needed on the genus-\(0\) path.} An earlier draft of
616	  this chapter recorded the theorem of the cube as a deferred prerequisite of the single-curve
617	  base case ``\(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) constant''. That was a misreading. The base case
618	  (Proposition~3.9, the \(d = 1\) case of Proposition~3.10) follows directly from the Rigidity
619	  Theorem~1.1 --- our \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} --- via Corollary~1.5 (additivity,
620	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) composed with the total \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling
621	  action \(\sigma_\times\) (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): the scaling fixed point \(0\) collapses one
622	  axis of the decomposition, yielding \(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\) and hence constancy on the dense
623	  \(\mathbb G_m\) (the primary \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut; see
624	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). This route does not even use the triviality of
625	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A)\) (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), which is retained only for the
626	  Milne-faithful \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive alternative. Neither the theorem of the cube nor Milne's Theorem~3.2 /
627	  Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension) is used: since \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is already a
628	  total morphism out of the complete \(\mathbb P^1\), no defect map needs extending over a complete
629	  surface (this corrects the earlier claim that the surface extension was on the genus-\(0\) path;
630	  see \cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) enters only for
631	  projectivity, the seesaw/square principle, dual abelian varieties, polarizations, and the
632	  Poincar\'e sheaf --- none on the genus-\(0\) path. For Route~A: the Albanese universal property
633	  that the positive-genus arm needs (Milne Proposition~6.1/6.4, \S III.6) is \emph{also} cube-free,
634	  resting on Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) + Corollary~1.2/1.5 --- which is
635	  precisely why Theorem~3.2 and Corollary~1.2 are retained in this chapter as Route-A inputs.
636	\end{remark}

665	  \(w_0 \in W\), and let \(p \colon V \times W \to V\), \(q \colon V \times W \to W\) be the

688	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
689	  Define \(f := h|_{V \times \{w_0\}}\) and \(g := h|_{\{v_0\} \times W}\), identifying
690	  \(V \times \{w_0\} \cong V\) and \(\{v_0\} \times W \cong W\); on points \(f(v) = h(v, w_0)\) and
691	  \(g(w) = h(v_0, w)\). Form the difference
692	  \[ \varphi := h - (f \circ p + g \circ q) \colon V \times W \to A, \qquad
693	     \varphi(v, w) = h(v, w) - h(v, w_0) - h(v_0, w) \]
694	  (the subtraction is in the group \(A\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is the composite of
695	  \((h, -(f \circ p + g \circ q))\) with the group law of \(A\)). Then \(\varphi\) vanishes on both
696	  axes: \(\varphi(V \times \{w_0\}) = 0 = \varphi(\{v_0\} \times W)\) (direct computation, using
697	  \(h(v_0, w_0) = 0\)). In particular \(\varphi\) collapses the complete axis \(V \times \{w_0\}\) to
698	  the single point \(0 \in A\), so by the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, with \(V\)
699	  complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically irreducible) \(\varphi\) factors through the projection
700	  \(q\); but it also vanishes on \(\{v_0\} \times W\), which is a section of \(q\), so the factoring
701	  morphism is identically \(0\), i.e.\ \(\varphi \equiv 0\). Hence \(h = f \circ p + g \circ q\).
702	  Uniqueness: restricting any such decomposition to the axes recovers \(f\) and \(g\) as the
703	  axis-restrictions of \(h\).
704	\end{proof}

741	  \leanok
742	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
743	  The image \(\alpha(0_A)\) is a \(\bar k\)-point \(b\) of \(B\); composing \(\alpha\) with translation by
744	  \(-b\) we may assume \(\alpha(0_A) = 0_B\), and it suffices to show such an \(\alpha\) is a
745	  homomorphism. Form
746	  \[ \varphi \colon A \times A \to B, \qquad \varphi(a, a') = \alpha(a + a') - \alpha(a) - \alpha(a'), \]
747	  the difference of the two regular maps \(A \times A \xrightarrow{m} A \xrightarrow{\alpha} B\)
748	  and \(A \times A \xrightarrow{\alpha \times \alpha} B \times B \xrightarrow{m} B\). Then
749	  \(\varphi(0_A, 0_A) = 0\) and \(\varphi\) vanishes on both axes,
750	  \(\varphi(A \times 0) = 0 = \varphi(0 \times A)\) (since \(\alpha(0) = 0\)). By the additive
751	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (with \(V = W = A\)), the axis-restrictions
752	  of \(\varphi\) being \(0\) force \(\varphi \equiv 0\); equivalently, \(\varphi\) collapses the complete
753	  axis \(A \times 0\) and vanishes on \(0 \times A\), so Rigidity gives \(\varphi = 0\). Hence
754	  \(\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')\), i.e.\ \(\alpha\) is a homomorphism.
755	\end{proof}

790	% Suppose φ is not defined at x. Then for some f ∈ Im(Φ*), (x,x) ∈ div(f)₁, and clearly Φ is
791	% not defined at the points (y,y) ∈ Δ ∩ div(f)₁. This is a subset of pure codimension one in Δ,

794	\begin{proof}
795	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem~3.2.}
796	  The statement is Theorem~3.1 combined with Lemma~3.3. By Theorem~3.1 (a consequence of the
797	  valuative criterion of properness, Mathlib \texttt{IsProper}/\texttt{ValuativeCriterion},
798	  applied at the discrete valuation ring of each codimension-\(1\) point), a rational map from the
799	  normal \(V\) to the complete \(A\) is defined outside a closed set of codimension \(\geq 2\). By
800	  Lemma~3.3, the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a \emph{group} variety is either
801	  empty or pure of codimension \(1\): forming the difference map
802	  \(\Phi(x, y) = \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow A\), one shows
803	  \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) iff \(\Phi\) is defined at the diagonal point \((x, x)\), and an
804	  indeterminacy point produces a pure-codimension-\(1\) component of the non-domain. The two
805	  facts are compatible only if the indeterminacy locus is empty; hence \(\alpha\) is everywhere
806	  defined.
807	\end{proof}
808	\begin{remark}
809	  \label{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}
810	  \textbf{Formalisation risk: the pure-codimension-\(1\) step (Lemma 3.3) has no Mathlib
811	  Weil-divisor support.} Theorem~3.1's everywhere-extension-outside-codimension-\(2\) is within
812	  reach of Mathlib's valuative criterion of properness
813	  (\texttt{ValuativeCriterion}/\texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}), applied at the
814	  discrete valuation ring \(\mathcal O_{V, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\). But Lemma~3.3,
815	  which rules out codimension-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety, is proved in
816	  Milne via Weil-divisor theory (\(\mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_1\), prime divisors,
817	  tangent cones) for which Mathlib currently has \emph{no} usable API at this generality. This
818	  is the route's riskiest sub-build. An open question for the prover: whether a \emph{pointwise}
819	  valuative extension --- showing, at each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) separately, that the
820	  morphism \(\Spec k(V) \to A\) extends over \(\Spec \mathcal O_{V, Z}\) using only that \(A\) is
821	  proper and a group (so the difference-map argument can be run \emph{locally} at \(Z\) without
822	  globally building \(\mathrm{div}(f)\)) --- side-steps building divisor theory entirely.
823	  \textbf{This surface extension is \emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) critical path (corrected, iter-164).}
824	  An earlier draft routed the genus-\(0\) base case through this extension, by collapsing the
825	  additive-defect map \(\psi(x, y) = f(x + y) - f(x) - f(y)\) on the \emph{affine} surface
826	  \(\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a\) and then extending \(\psi\) over the complete surface
827	  \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) --- which genuinely \emph{would} need Theorem~3.2's surface case
828	  (Milne Theorem~3.4 / Lemma~3.3). That detour is unnecessary. The 4th route
829	  (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}) replaces the two-variable affine defect map by the
830	  \emph{total} translation action \(\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1\)
831	  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): \(h := \sigma \fatsemi f\) is a total morphism on $\mathbb P^1
832	  \times \mathbb G_a\( (no extension needed), and the complete-source factor is \)\mathbb P^1$
833	  itself, so the additive decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} applies directly.
834	  Consequently \cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Theorem~3.2), the codim-\(1\) indeterminacy
835	  sub-build (Lemma~3.3), and the Weil-divisor gap discussed above are all confined to
836	  \textbf{Route~A} (the positive-genus Albanese universal property, Milne \S III.6); they are
837	  retained in this chapter solely as Route-A inputs and may be deferred until Route~A consumes
838	  them. The genus-\(0\) close no longer depends on any of them.
839	\end{remark}

841	\begin{definition}

843	  \label{def:genus0_base_objects}

846	  % I.2--3 for the projective line; the group laws on \(\mathbb A^1\) are elementary). No external
847	  % % SOURCE QUOTE is needed for the Lean encoding itself.
848	  Work over \(\Spec \bar k\) with \(\bar k\) algebraically closed. The primary \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling
849	  route (and its \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive alternative) needs only three concrete objects of
850	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\):
851	  \begin{itemize}
852	    \item \(\mathbb P^1 = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), the projective line --- a smooth proper
853	      geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\) (Mathlib: projective space, or \(\mathrm{Proj}\)
854	      of the graded polynomial ring; \lean name \texttt{ProjectiveLineBar}). It carries
855	      the distinguished \(\bar k\)-points \(0\), \(1\), \(\infty\) and the two standard affine charts
856	      \(\mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\) (coordinate \(x\)) and
857	      \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0\}\) (coordinate \(u = 1/x\)).
858	    \item \(\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k}\), the affine line as the \emph{additive} group object
859	      (Mathlib: \texttt{AffineSpace}\,\(\mathbb A(1; \Spec \bar k)\) with the \(\mathtt{GrpObj}\)
860	      additive structure \((x, y) \mapsto x + y\), identity \(0\); \lean name \texttt{Ga}).
861	      As a scheme \(\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\), and the open
862	      immersion \(\mathbb G_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb P^1\) has dense (cofinite) image.
863	    \item \(\mathbb G_m = \mathbb A^1 \setminus \{0\} = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0, \infty\}\), the
864	      \emph{multiplicative} group object (group law \((x, y) \mapsto xy\), identity \(1\); \lean name
865	      \texttt{Gm}). Its open immersion into \(\mathbb P^1\) also has dense image.
866	  \end{itemize}
867	  All three are of finite type over \(\bar k\); \(\mathbb P^1\) is proper, while \(\mathbb G_a\) and
868	  \(\mathbb G_m\) are affine (hence not complete). These are the only ``genus-\(0\)'' geometric inputs
869	  the base case needs.
870	\end{definition}

1061	  For each \(i \in \{0, 1\}\), the ring map
1062	  \[ \mathrm{mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway}_i \colon
1063	     \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k \longrightarrow
1064	     \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i \]
1065	  --- the inverse direction of the chart-ring iso of \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso},
1066	  built via \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial.eval}_2\mathrm{Hom}\) as the ring map sending the
1067	  generator \(u \in \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\) to the homogeneous-localisation
1068	  element \(X_{1-i}/X_i \in \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) and the constants
1069	  \(r \in \bar k\) along the composite $\bar k \to \mathcal A_0 \to \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal
1070	  A\,X_i\( --- is surjective onto \)\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$.
1071	  \medskip
1072	  \textit{Proof.} The image of \(\mathrm{mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway}_i\)
1073	  is the \(\mathcal A_0\)-subalgebra of \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) generated by the
1074	  single localised element \(X_{1-i}/X_i\), since \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\)
1075	  is generated as a \(\bar k\)-algebra by its single generator \(u\) and the base-ring map
1076	  \(\bar k \to \mathcal A_0\) is bijective (via the canonical identification of degree-zero
1077	  scalars in the \(\bar k\)-algebra \(\bar k[X_0, X_1]\) with \(\bar k\)). Mathlib's lemma
1078	  \(\mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away.adjoin\_mk\_prod\_pow\_eq\_top}\) says that for any
1079	  multi-index \(v \colon \iota' \to A\) of homogeneous elements of degree \(dv\) generating
1080	  \(\mathcal A\) up to degree \(d\), the \(\mathcal A_0\)-subalgebra of
1081	  \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,(\prod v_i^{dv_i})\) generated by the family of ratios
1082	  \(\{v_i^{dv_i}/\prod v_j^{dv_j}\}\) is the whole ring \(\top\). Specialising to
1083	  \(d = 1\), \(\iota' = \mathrm{Fin}\,2\), \(v = (X_0, X_1)\), and exponent vector
1084	  \(dv = (1, 1)\), the relevant generator \(\prod v_i^{dv_i} = X_0 \cdot X_1\) collapses
1085	  along the localisation at \(X_i\) (the \(X_i\)-component is already a unit, so only the
1086	  \(X_{1-i}/X_i\) ratio remains as a non-trivial generator), and the conclusion is that
1087	  \(\mathrm{Algebra.adjoin}\,\mathcal A_0\,\{X_{1-i}/X_i\} = \top\) inside
1088	  \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\). This is exactly the image of
1089	  \(\mathrm{mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway}_i\), so the map is surjective. \qed
1090	\end{lemma}

1193	  \textbf{The scaling action (primary).} The \emph{multiplicative scaling action}
1194	  \[ \sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1195	     \text{``}\sigma_\times(x, \lambda) = \lambda x\text{''} \]
1196	  (\lean name \texttt{gmScalingP1}) is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of
1197	  \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m\) --- no indeterminacy, characteristic-free (M\"obius scaling
1198	  fixing both \(0\) and \(\infty\)) --- defined chartwise:
1199	  \begin{itemize}
1200	    \item On \(\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_m\) (target chart \(x\)): \((x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\),
1201	      a polynomial map, regular everywhere.
1202	    \item Near \(\infty\) (target chart \(u = 1/x\)): the target coordinate \(1/(\lambda x)\) rewrites as
1203	      \(u/\lambda\), regular everywhere on this chart because \(\lambda \in \mathbb G_m\) is invertible.
1204	  \end{itemize}
1205	  The two target charts cover the image and overlap consistently, so \(\sigma_\times\) is defined on
1206	  the whole surface. It is a left action of the group \(\mathbb G_m\) on \(\mathbb P^1\):
1207	  \(\sigma_\times(x, 1) = x\) and $\sigma_\times(\sigma_\times(x, \lambda), \mu) = \sigma_\times(x,
1208	  \lambda\mu)\(. Crucially it has \emph{two fixed points}, \)\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ and
1209	  \(\sigma_\times(\infty, \lambda) = \infty\) for all \(\lambda\); the fixed point \(0\) is what makes the
1210	  \(W\)-axis of the additive decomposition collapse in the primary base-case proof
1211	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}).

1220	  coordinate \(1/(x + y)\) rewrites as \(u/(1 + u y)\), regular at \(u = 0\) (value \(0\) there --- \(\sigma\)
1221	  fixes \(\infty\) for every \(y\)), while the locus \(x + y = 0\) is covered by the chart \(x\) on which it
1222	  is the finite value \(0\). It is a left action of \(\mathbb G_a\) on \(\mathbb P^1\) fixing \(\infty\):
1223	  \(\sigma(x, 0) = x\) and \(\sigma(\sigma(x, y), y') = \sigma(x, y + y')\). This companion underlies
1224	  only the alternative proof of \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} and the demoted $\mathbb
1225	  G_a$-additive close of the base case (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}); it is \emph{not} on the
1226	  genus-\(0\) critical path.
1227	\end{definition}

4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}

15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same

95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of

99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	\begin{definition}
102	
103	
104	\leanok
105	[Line bundle of a closed point]
106	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
108	  
109	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
110	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
111	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
113	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
114	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
115	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
116	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
117	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
118	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
119	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
120	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
121	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
122	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
123	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
124	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
125	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
126	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
127	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
128	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
129	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
130	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
131	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
132	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
133	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
134	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
135	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
136	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
137	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
138	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
139	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
140	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
141	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
142	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
143	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
144	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
145	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
146	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
147	
148	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
149	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
150	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
151	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
152	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
153	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
154	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
155	  \[
156	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
157	    \mathcal O_C)
158	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
159	  \]
160	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
161	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
162	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
163	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
164	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
165	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
166	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
167	
168	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
169	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
170	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
171	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
172	  REF), and returns an invertible
173	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
174	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
175	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
176	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
177	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
178	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
179	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
180	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}

196	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
197	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
198	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
199	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
200	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
201	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
202	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
203	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
204	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
205	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
206	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
207	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
208	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient
209	  rings \(\mathcal K_C\). Since \(C\) is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the
210	  constant sheaf at the function field \(K(C)\), and taking global sections
211	  of the structural inclusion \(\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\)
212	  produces a canonical \(\bar k\)-linear map
213	  \[
214	    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
215	      \;\longrightarrow\; H^0(C, \mathcal K_C) \;\cong\; K(C)
216	  \]
217	  sending a global section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to the rational
218	  function on \(C\) obtained by composing \(s\) with the inclusion of
219	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into \(\mathcal K_C\) at each point. The map \(\iota\) is
220	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
221	  \mathcal K_C\( is torsion-free of rank \)1$, so taking global sections
222	  preserves injectivity on the integral scheme \(C\)).
223	
224	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
225	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
226	  takes the same inputs as REF (a curve
227	  \(C\), a point \(P\), and the closedness witness
228	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C.left)}) and produces, for each global
229	  section \(s\) of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) at twist \(0\) in the project's
230	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured global-sections pipeline, the corresponding
231	  element \(\iota(s) \in K(C)\) of the project's function-field carrier
232	  \texttt{C.left.functionField}. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}
233	  and its body is gated on the body of
234	  REF.
235	\end{definition}

237	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
238	
239	\begin{lemma}
240	
241	
242	\leanok
243	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
244	controlled pole]
245	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
247	  
248	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
249	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
250	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
251	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
252	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
253	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
255	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
256	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
257	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
258	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
259	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
260	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
261	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
262	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
263	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
264	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
265	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
266	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
267	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
268	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
269	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
270	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
271	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
272	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
273	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
274	  Riemann--Roch space
275	  \[
276	    L([P]) \;:=\;
277	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
278	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
279	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
280	  \]
281	  Equivalently, the order conditions
282	  \[
283	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
284	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
285	    \qquad
286	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
287	  \]
288	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
289	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
290	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
291	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
292	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
293	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
294	
295	  Under this identification, the inclusion
296	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
297	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
298	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
299	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
300	\end{lemma}
301	
302	\begin{proof}
303	  
304	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
305	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
306	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
307	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
308	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
309	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
310	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
311	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
312	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
313	  and only if:
314	
315	  \begin{itemize}
316	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
317	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
318	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
319	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
320	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
321	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
322	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
323	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
324	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
325	  \end{itemize}
326	  In divisorial language (REF,
327	  REF), the two conditions combine to
328	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
329	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
330	  zero global section.
331	
332	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
333	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
334	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
335	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
336	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
337	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
338	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
339	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
340	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
341	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
342	\end{proof}

344	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}
347	
348	
349	\leanok
350	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
351	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
353	  
354	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
355	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
356	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
357	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
358	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
359	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
360	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
361	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
362	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
363	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
364	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
365	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
366	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
367	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
368	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
369	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
370	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
371	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
372	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
373	  \[
374	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
375	  \]
376	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
377	\end{lemma}
378	
379	\begin{proof}
380	  
381	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
382	  exact sequence
383	  \[
384	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
385	  \]
386	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
387	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
388	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
389	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
390	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
391	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
392	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
393	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
394	  \[
395	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
396	  \]
397	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
398	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
399	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
400	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
401	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
402	  \begin{multline*}
403	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
404	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
405	  \end{multline*}
406	  Two ingredients close the proof:
407	
408	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
409	  hypothesis: by REF,
410	  \[
411	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
412	  \]
413	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
414	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
415	
416	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
417	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
418	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
419	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
420	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
421	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
422	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
423	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
424	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
425	
426	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
427	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
428	  \[
429	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
430	  \]
431	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
432	
433	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
434	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
435	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
436	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
437	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
438	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
439	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
440	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
441	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
442	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
443	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
444	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
445	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
446	  complex degenerates).
447	\end{proof}
448	
449	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
450	
451	\begin{theorem}
452	
453	
454	\leanok
455	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
456	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
457	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
458	  
459	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
460	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
461	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
462	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
463	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
464	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
465	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
466	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
467	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
468	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
469	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
470	  % is the Euler characteristic
471	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
472	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
473	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
474	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
475	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
476	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
477	  \[
478	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
479	  \]
480	\end{theorem}
481	
482	\begin{proof}
483	  
484	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
485	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
486	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
487	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
488	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
489	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
490	  \[
491	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
492	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
493	  \]
494	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
495	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
496	  \[
497	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
498	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
499	    \;=\; 2.
500	  \]
501	  By
502	  REF, the second
503	  term vanishes:
504	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
505	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
506	
507	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
508	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
509	  REF computes
510	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
511	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
512	  \[
513	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
514	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
515	  \]
516	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
517	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
518	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
519	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
520	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
521	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
522	  segment yields the short exact sequence
523	  \[
524	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
525	  \]
526	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
527	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
528	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
529	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
530	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
531	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
532	\end{proof}
533	
534	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
535	
536	\begin{corollary}
537	
538	
539	\leanok
540	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
541	single simple pole]
542	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
543	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
544	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
545	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
549	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
550	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
551	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
552	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
554	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
555	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
556	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
557	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
558	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
559	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
560	  \begin{itemize}
561	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
562	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
563	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
564	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
565	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
566	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
567	  \end{itemize}
568	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
569	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
570	\end{corollary}
571	
572	\begin{proof}
573	  
574	  By
575	  REF,
576	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
577	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
578	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
579	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
580	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
581	  identification of
582	  REF), is a one-dimensional
583	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
584	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
585	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
586	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
587	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
588	
589	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
590	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
591	  REF the section
592	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
593	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
594	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
595	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
596	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
597	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
598	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
599	  of the statement are now immediate.
600	
601	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
602	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
603	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
604	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
605	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
606	
607	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
608	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
609	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
610	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
611	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
612	  REF's output. The
613	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
614	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
615	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
616	\end{proof}
617	
618	\section{Mathlib-status flag: the H\(^1\)-vanishing input}
619	
620	The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve
621	(REF) is the
622	critical cohomological input of this chapter. Its closure depends on
623	two Mathlib-status facts, both of which the project already consumes
624	elsewhere:
625	
626	\begin{itemize}
627	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence of \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf
628	    cohomology.} Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships
629	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact} for
630	    \(\Ext^*\)-long-exact-sequences at the abelian-group level. The
631	    project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured pipeline
632	    (REF, \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex})
633	    inherits it by forget-functor naturality. This bridge is already
634	    consumed by REF of
635	    \texttt{RR.2}, so closing it here introduces no new Mathlib
636	    obligation.
637	  \item \textbf{Vanishing of higher cohomology of a skyscraper sheaf}
638	    on a Noetherian scheme. Hartshorne~III.2.5 gives the flasque
639	    formulation; the project's affine-cover Čech route
640	    (REF and surrounding
641	    \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex} machinery) computes
642	    the same vanishing for the skyscraper at a closed point by direct
643	    Čech-degeneration. \texttt{RR.2}'s inductive step already invokes
644	    \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), which is the combined statement
645	    \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and \(\dim H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\),
646	    so the vanishing \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) is already a consumer
647	    obligation of \texttt{RR.2}.
648	\end{itemize}
649	
650	Consequently the H\(^1\)-vanishing
651	REF introduces no
652	Mathlib obligation that is not already on \texttt{RR.2}'s critical
653	path. The genus-\(0\) specialisation should therefore close at the
654	formalisation stage without splitting this chapter or pushing the
655	vanishing back into \texttt{RR.2}.

7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

63	% to a 3-field structure

65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`

73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence

76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  
93	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
96	  % "In Situation REF.
97	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
98	  %  \(\)
99	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
100	  %  \(\)
101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}
103	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
104	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
105	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
106	  %  \(\)
107	  %  \xymatrix{
108	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  \(\)
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
115	  %  \end{enumerate}
116	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over \(X\).
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
138	% Lemmas REF,
139	% REF, and
140	% REF.
141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
142	% Lemma REF."
143	\begin{proof}
144	  
145	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
146	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
147	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
148	  open-immersion transition data
149	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
150	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
151	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
153	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
154	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
155	\end{proof}

157	\begin{theorem}\leanok
158	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
159	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
160	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}
161	  
162	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
163	  % structural consequence ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than

168	  % a functor'', functor F and Lemma lemma-spec
169	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L427-L466 + L547-L551).
170	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

172	  %  \(f^*\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebras.
173	  %  We are going to consider pairs \((f : T \to S, \varphi)\) where
174	  %  \(f\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)
175	  %  is a morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebras. Note that this is the

182	  %  We will use all three ways of thinking about \(\varphi\),

184	  %  In this way we have defined a functor

193	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
194	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
195	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
196	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
197	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
198	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;

219	  \leanok
220	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
221	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
222	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /

230	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
231	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
232	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
233	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
234	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
235	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
236	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case

246	\section{Affine base}
247	\label{sec:relspec_affine}
248	
249	\begin{theorem}\leanok
250	[Relative spectrum over an affine base]
251	  \label{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}
253	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
254	  % weaker ``IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)'' rather than the canonical
255	  % iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))'' pinned by this prose. The Lean name
256	  % ``affine_base_iff'' is also misleading (the encoded type is not an iff).
257	  % Iter-174+ refines to the iff-with-Γ statement once Γ : QcohAlgebra → CommRingCat
258	  % is available.
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
261	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
262	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
263	  % "In Situation REF.
264	  %  Let \(F\) be the functor associated to \((S, \mathcal{A})\) above.
265	  %  If \(S\) is affine, then \(F\) is representable by the
266	  %  affine scheme \(\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))\)."
267	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine).}
268	  Let \(X = \Spec R\) be an affine scheme and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
269	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an
270	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
271	  \[
272	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
273	  \]
274	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
275	  spectrum of the global sections.
276	\end{theorem}
277	
278	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write \(S = \Spec(R)\) and \(A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A})\).
279	% Then \(A\) is an \(R\)-algebra and \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde A\).
280	% The ring map \(R \to A\) gives rise to a canonical map
281	% \(\)

283	% \longrightarrow
284	% S = \Spec(R).

485	\section{Lean encoding}
486	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
487	
488	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
489	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
490	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
491	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
492	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
493	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
494	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
495	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
496	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
497	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
498	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
499	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
500	(Theorem~REF) is expressed as a
501	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
502	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
503	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
504	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
505	(Theorem~REF) and functoriality
506	(Theorem~REF) are derived from the universal property by
507	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
508	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.
509

512	
513	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its three core
514	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
515	are explicitly out of scope here:

520	  \item \textbf{Line-bundle pullback on a product \(C \times_k T\)} (A.1.b) ---
521	    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).
522	  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)}
523	    --- downstream properties that depend on assumptions on \(\mathcal{A}\)

526	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.
527	\end{itemize}

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.

2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring
8	% \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay'' that A.4.a (the codim-1 extension proof for
9	% Albanese morphisms out of a regular projective surface) consumes. Per
10	% STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
11	% startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the
12	% Albanese geometry and lives entirely under \texttt{RingTheory.*}. The
13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}
14	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit
20	% at the pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) should confirm what is present
21	% before the prover writes the file, since a fully Mathlib-backed
22	% Auslander--Buchsbaum would collapse this chapter to a re-export.
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
