1	\chapter{Rigidity over a base field \(k\): morphisms from a genus-\(0\) curve to a group scheme are constant}
2	\label{chap:RigidityKbar}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityKbar.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157): The project's COMMITTED genus-0 rigidity route is now route
6	% (c) --- the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity-lemma stack developed in its own
7	% chapter \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (Lean file
8	% AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, upstream of Jacobian.lean). The headline
9	% there, thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV (char-free, [IsAlgClosed k-bar] only), is what
10	% genusZeroWitness consumes. THIS chapter now documents the over-k-bar declaration
11	% rigidity_over_kbar (which carries [CharZero k-bar]) together with the FALLBACK route (a):
12	% the df=0 / cotangent-triviality + Serre-duality decomposition and the closed, axiom-clean
13	% chart-algebra envelope (which supplies only the converse "df=0 implies constant"). Route
14	% (a) is retained in tree, off the critical path; the df=0 framing below is the fallback,
15	% NOT the committed route.
16	
17	This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-\(0\) Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero} (the alg-closed pivot of \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot" below; descent of the constancy conclusion to a general base field \(k\) is a separate downstream step, hosted in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is
18	
19	\begin{quote}
20	  \emph{Let \(A\) be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). Then every morphism \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant.}
21	\end{quote}
22	
23	This is the keystone classical input for the C.2.d sub-step of Jacobian.tex \S C.2: the genus-\(0\) universal-property step of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} that concludes the universal-pointed-morphism factorisation by ruling out positive-dimensional images of \(f \colon C \to A\) when \(C\) has genus \(0\) and a \(k\)-rational marked point.
24	
25	\paragraph{Disposition (iter-155): \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} is a gated NAMED GAP.} The named declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} is landed with a \texttt{sorry} body and is held as a \textbf{named gap} in the same family as \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Jacobian.tex) and the deferred Serre-duality / Riemann--Roch stack --- honestly disclosed, not on a prover lane. The substantive content (the C.2.d sub-step below, ruling out a \(1\)-dimensional image of \(f\)) reduces to the vanishing of the cotangent pull-back \(\mathrm df \colon f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{C/\bar k}\), and a decisive iter-155 \texttt{mathlib-analogist} consult (\texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) established that \textbf{producing \(\mathrm df = 0\) is irreducibly a global-sections fact}, gated on two genuinely-absent inputs:
26	
27	\begin{itemize}
28	  \item \textbf{(gap i) Global cotangent-triviality of \(A\):} \(\Omega_{A/\bar k} \cong \mathcal O_A^{\oplus g}\), generated by translation-invariant differentials. The presheaf globalisation that would build this (\texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) was \textbf{excised from the Lean tree at iter-145}; \texttt{GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} \(+\) \texttt{shearMulRight} survive only as a \emph{design template}. Estimated \(\sim 800\)--\(1500\) LOC. Mathlib's only ``group cotangent is trivial'' idiom is the smooth-manifold \texttt{GroupLieAlgebra} stack, which does not port to schemes.
29	  \item \textbf{(gap ii) Serre duality \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\)} for the genus-\(0\) curve. Given the global trivialisation of gap (i), \(\mathrm df \in H^0\bigl(C, \mathcal{H}om(f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr) \cong \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\). Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has no dualizing sheaf and no Riemann--Roch; the project's \texttt{Cohomology/*} stack computes \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (genus) but has \textbf{no \(H^0\)-vanishing lemma and no genus\(\leftrightarrow\Omega\) bridge}. The genus-\(0\) hypothesis \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) does \emph{not} yield \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) without the duality. Estimated \(\sim 3000\)--\(8000\) LOC.
30	\end{itemize}
31	
32	\noindent The chart-algebra envelope of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM), \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}, and the delegate \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}), which \emph{is} closed and axiom-clean, supplies only the \textbf{converse} half of the bridge: ``if \(\mathrm df = 0\) then \(f\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) (is constant)''. It \emph{cannot} produce \(\mathrm df = 0\): on each affine chart \(V \subseteq C\), \(\Omega_{C/\bar k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero), so a chart-by-chart computation never detects the vanishing --- the vanishing materialises only on global sections. Earlier framings in this chapter (the piece-(iv) ``DEFERRED, not on the C.2.d critical path'' disposition and the ``No-Serre-duality layer'') asserted that the chart-algebra route \emph{avoids} Serre duality; \textbf{that is false}, and is corrected throughout \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) is \emph{on} the critical path for producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), not avoided.
33	
34	The project does \textbf{not} open a ``produce \(\mathrm df = 0\)'' prover lane until one of two candidate closure routes is built; the decision between them is a leading open strategic question and the chapter commits to neither:
35	\begin{itemize}
36	  \item \textbf{Route (a):} build gaps (i) \(+\) (ii) directly.
37	  \item \textbf{Route (b):} the dual-abelian-variety keystone --- ``morphisms \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) are constant because \(\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0\)''. Earlier rejected as ``needs \(\Pic^0\)'', but since the positive-genus arm of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Route A) needs the \(\Pic^0\) engine \emph{regardless}, route (b) may amortise the shared machinery and be globally cheaper than a standalone Serre-duality build.
38	\end{itemize}
39	
40	\paragraph{Iter-152 alg-closed pivot.} The project committed an architectural pivot: rigidity is proved \textbf{only over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero}, and the constancy conclusion descends to a general base field \(k\) downstream. The reason is that the chart-algebra core lemma \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM) is mathematically \emph{false} as a bare \(B\)-only algebra lemma: a prover lane exhibited two counterexamples that satisfy every hypothesis yet have \(\ker D \supsetneq \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap})\) --- namely \(B = k \times k\) (finite \'etale over \(k\)) and \(B = \mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)\) over \(\mathbb Q\). The missing content is precisely ``\(k\) algebraically closed in \(B\)''. Adding the \emph{joint} hypotheses \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[IsDomain B]} makes it true: \texttt{[IsDomain B]} kills the \(B = k \times k\) counterexample and \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} kills the \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)/\mathbb Q\) counterexample. (Neither hypothesis suffices alone; the iter-151 note that ``\texttt{[IsDomain B]} alone fails'' considered the hypotheses only one-at-a-time.) Over an algebraically closed base the \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} constants lemma also collapses to a one-liner via \texttt{IsAlgClosed.algebraMap\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}, and the entire (S3.\textsc{sep}/\textsc{pi}) base-change-to-\(\bar k\) chain is descoped from the M2.a critical path.

13	\[

35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are

38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}

1483	  \label{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1484	  
1485	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1486	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)
1487	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.9. Every rational map A¹ ⇢ A or P¹ ⇢ A is constant."

1492	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1493	  \textbf{Off the genus-\(0\) critical path (iter-164).} This \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\)
1494	  lemma is \emph{superseded by the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut}
1495	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}), which closes the
1496	  base case without it. It is retained only as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9 ($\mathbb
1497	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility) argument feeding the alternative route
1498	  \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}; no block on the genus-\(0\) critical path depends on it.
1499	  Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
1500	  \(\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A\) be a homomorphism of group objects (so \(\varphi(0) = 0_A\) and
1501	  \(\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)\)). Then \(\varphi\) is the trivial homomorphism: it
1502	  factors through the terminal object as the constant morphism at the identity,
1503	  \(\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A\).
1504	  \medskip
1505	  \noindent\textbf{Equivalent completeness phrasing.} Equivalently: a homomorphism from an
1506	  \emph{affine connected} group object into a \emph{complete} group object is trivial. That is the
1507	  abstract content; the proof below realises it characteristic-free.
1508	\end{lemma}
1509	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1510	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1511	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1512	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1513	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1514	\begin{proof}
1515	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1516	  Write \(A\) additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1517	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1518	  G_a/\mathbb G_m\( incompatibility, which applies whenever \)\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1519	  \(\mathbb P^1\) (as it does in the genus-\(0\) application).
1520	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image \(\varphi(\mathbb G_a)\) is a connected subgroup of \(A\)
1521	  (image of the connected \(\mathbb G_a\) under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1522	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of \(A\), hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1523	  complete \(A\)); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} \(\mathbb G_a\) it is moreover
1524	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over \(\bar k\) is a
1525	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over \(\bar k\), hence equal
1526	  to \(\bar k\) (the \(\Gamma = \bar k\) collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1527	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore \(\varphi(\mathbb G_a)\) is one point;
1528	  being a homomorphism, \(\varphi\) sends \(0\) there, so the point is \(0_A\) and
1529	  \(\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A\).
1530	  \medskip
1531	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility (when \(\varphi\)
1532	  extends to \(\mathbb P^1\)).} Suppose \(\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) for a morphism
1533	  \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) (the genus-\(0\) situation). Set \(g := f - f(1)\), so \(g(1) = 0_A\), and
1534	  form \(h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A\) with the
1535	  scaling companion \(\sigma_\times\) of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1536	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) (complete),
1537	  \(W = \mathbb G_m\), base points \(1 \in \mathbb P^1\) and \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\) (so
1538	  \(h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A\)), the axis restrictions are \(x \mapsto g(x)\) and
1539	  \(\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)\), whence \(g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)\) on \(\bar k^\times\);
1540	  re-expanding \(g\) gives the multiplicative relation
1541	  \(\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c\) with \(c = -\varphi(1)\). Now \(\varphi\) is at once
1542	  additive, \(\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)\), and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1543	  \(\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c\). Computing \(\varphi((x + x')\,y)\) in two ways for
1544	  \(x, x' \in \bar k\), \(y \in \bar k^\times\),
1545	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1546	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1547	  \varphi(y) + c\(; equating gives \)\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)\( for every \)y \in \bar k^\times$.
1548	  Hence \(\varphi\) is constant on the dense \(\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a\), so constant on
1549	  \(\mathbb G_a\) (density, \(A\) separated), with value \(\varphi(0) = 0_A\). This is Milne's argument
1550	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1551	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1552	\end{proof}

1562	  on the affine \(\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a\) and must be extended over $\mathbb P^1 \times

1792	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-\(0\) curve}
1793	\begin{theorem}
1794	\leanok
1795	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-\(0\) curve to an abelian variety is constant]
1796	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
1797	  
1798	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
1799	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
1800	  \(\mathtt{CharZero}\) hypothesis). Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1801	  over \(\bar k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\), and let \(A\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
1802	  group scheme over \(\bar k\) (an abelian variety) with identity \(\eta_A\). Then for every
1803	  morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) such that \(p \fatsemi f = \eta_A\) for some \(\bar k\)-point
1804	  \(p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C\), the morphism \(f\) equals the constant morphism at the identity,
1805	  \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A\).
1806	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-\(0\)
1807	  Albanese-witness \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}\) consumes. Its signature mirrors
1808	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}\) of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
1809	  \emph{verbatim except} that the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) instance is dropped: same
1810	  curve typeclasses (\(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\),
1811	  \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\) on \(C\)), same abelian-variety typeclasses
1812	  (\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\)
1813	  on \(A\)), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
1814	  \eta[A]$.
1815	\end{theorem}
1816	\begin{proof}
1817	  \leanok
1818	  By \cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1} the genus-\(0\) curve \(C\) over the algebraically closed
1819	  field \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via some isomorphism
1820	  \(\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). The composite
1821	  \(f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is then a morphism from
1822	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) to the abelian variety \(A\), hence constant by
1823	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single \(\bar k\)-point \(a_0 \in A\).
1824	  Therefore \(f\) itself is constant with value \(a_0\). The pointed hypothesis
1825	  \(p \fatsemi f = \eta_A\) evaluates this constant at the \(\bar k\)-point \(p\) and pins it to the
1826	  identity, \(a_0 = \eta_A\). Consequently \(f\) factors through the terminal object as the
1827	  constant morphism at the identity, \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A\).
1828	  No differential (\(df = 0\)) input, no \(H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0\) / Serre-duality decomposition,
1829	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
1830	  Once formalised it lets the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) hypothesis carried by
1831	  \(\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}\) be dropped, and (after the \(\bar k \to k\) descent step hosted
1832	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}\).
1833	  % NOTE (iter-166 review): proof body landed iter-166 via clean iso-transport
1834	  % (`obtain ⟨φ⟩ := genusZero_curve_iso_P1 _hgenus; set g := φ.inv ≫ f; obtain ⟨a₀, hga₀⟩ :=
1835	  % morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const g; pin a₀ = η[A] via `toUnit_unique` basepoint transport;
1836	  % back-transport via `φ.hom_inv_id`'). `lean_verify` axioms $\{\mathtt{propext},
1837	  % \mathtt{sorryAx}, \mathtt{Classical.choice}, \mathtt{Quot.sound}\}\( — \)\mathtt{sorryAx}$
1838	  % propagates honestly through (a) `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (RR-bridge sub-build, deferred to
1839	  % iter-167+) and (b) `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const`'s 5 helper sorries. Lifts to axiom-clean
1840	  % once both upstreams discharge; checker confirmed no laundering.
1841	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The rational-curve isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) (RR.4)}
2	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}
3	\uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections, lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso, cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero, thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero, def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
4	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)

7	% + I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field \(\Leftrightarrow\) smooth-projective-curve

11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	This chapter is the fourth and final sub-build chapter
13	\texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4} closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
14	\[
15	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
16	  \,\colon\,
17	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
18	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
19	\]
20	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
21	\begin{itemize}
22	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
23	    group \(\Div(C)\), the degree map
24	    \(\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z\) (\texttt{def:divisor_degree}), and the
25	    degree-zero principal-divisor identity
26	    (\texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero}).
27	  \item \texttt{RR.2} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}): the Riemann--Roch
28	    formula in genus zero \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\)
29	    (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
30	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
31	    the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\), its global-sections identification
32	    \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2\), and the corollary
33	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
34	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
35	    \bar k \cdot 1\( on a genus-\)0\( curve with a marked \)\bar k$-point.
36	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
37	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\)
38	    and a \(\bar k\)-rational point is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).''
39	\end{itemize}
40	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
41	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
42	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as
43	the interface block \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
44	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}. The \texttt{RR.4}-closure of that target
45	also drops the \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
46	\texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme} via
47	\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}.
48	\paragraph{Scope of the \(\bar k\)-base classification.} The chapter restricts
49	to the case where the base field \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
50	k\( every closed point is \)\bar k\(-rational, so the ``\)C\( has a \)\bar
51	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
52	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) is non-empty,
53	hence has closed points, all of which are \(\bar k\)-rational). The general
54	``\(k \to \bar k\) descent'' step ``genus-\(0\) curve over a non-closed \(k\) with
55	a \(k\)-rational point \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' is a separate sub-build hosted
56	in \texttt{Jacobian.tex} (the \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key} downstream
57	consumer) and is out of scope here.
58	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
59	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
60	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signature
61	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) threads the
62	project's standing curve typeclass package:
63	\begin{quote}
64	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
65	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
66	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
67	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]}
68	\end{quote}
69	matching the typeclass set used by the headline target
70	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} in
71	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290--296}. The genus
72	\(g(C)\) is the one defined in \texttt{def:genus} of \texttt{Genus.tex},
73	namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) via the project's
74	\(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
75	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}); the concrete projective line target
76	\(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is the project's
77	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar~kbar} of

79	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
80	\begin{lemma}
81	\leanok
82	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
83	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
84	  
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
86	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
87	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
88	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
89	  \[
90	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
91	  \]
92	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
93	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
94	  \[
95	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
96	  \]
97	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
98	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
99	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
100	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
101	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
102	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
103	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
104	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
105	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
106	\end{lemma}
107	\begin{proof}
108	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
109	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
110	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
111	  under the name
112	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
113	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
114	  \begin{quote}
115	  \small
116	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
117	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
118	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
119	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
120	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
121	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
122	  \end{quote}
123	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
124	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
125	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
126	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
127	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
128	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
129	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
130	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
131	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
132	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
133	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
134	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
135	  project-bespoke wrapper
136	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
137	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
138	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
139	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
140	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
141	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
142	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
143	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
144	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
145	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
146	\end{proof}
147	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
148	% NOTE (iter-182 plan-phase Pin 2 signature strengthening). The iter-177
149	% file-skeleton placeholder existential
150	%   "$\exists (d : \mathbb N) (D : C.\mathrm{WeilDivisor}),\
151	%      0 < d \wedge D.\mathrm{degree} = d$"
152	% was vacuous in \(\varphi\) (no constraint tying \(D\) to \(\varphi\) or the
153	% point \(\infty\)). The iter-182 plan-phase refactor (refactor slug
154	% `pin2-sig-strengthen`) replaces it with the substantive existential
155	% pinning \(D\) to \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) and the degree to the function-field
156	% extension degree \([K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\). The new typed-sorry def
157	% `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` (in
158	% `RationalCurveIso.lean`, analogous to the iter-181 typed-sorry def
159	% `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField` of OCofP.lean) carries the
160	% Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\); its body is queued for iter-183+
161	% via the affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route.
162	\begin{lemma}
163	\leanok
164	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
165	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
166	  
167	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
168	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
169	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
170	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
171	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
172	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
173	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
175	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
176	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
177	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
178	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
179	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
180	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
181	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
182	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
183	  \[
184	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
185	    \quad\text{and}\quad
186	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
187	  \]
188	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
189	  \[
190	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
191	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
192	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
193	  \]
194	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
195	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
196	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
197	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
198	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
199	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
200	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
201	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
202	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
203	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
204	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
205	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
206	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
207	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
208	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
209	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
210	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
211	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
212	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
213	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
214	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
215	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
216	\end{lemma}
217	\begin{proof}
218	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
219	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
220	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
221	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
222	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
223	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
224	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
225	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
226	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
227	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
228	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
229	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
230	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
231	  is finite.
232	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
233	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
234	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
235	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
236	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
237	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
238	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
239	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
240	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
241	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
242	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
243	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
244	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
245	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
246	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
247	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
248	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
249	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
250	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
251	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
252	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
253	\end{proof}
254	\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
255	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
256	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
257	% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
258	% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to \(\varphi\)) was strictly
259	% weaker than the birational-extension body needs and was refined in
260	% iter-181 to the explicit function-field-extension-degree formulation
261	% [K(C):K(C')] = 1, i.e. Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField
262	% = 1, with C.functionField a C'.functionField-algebra via the canonical
263	% \varphi-induced map (Scheme.Hom.stalkMap at the generic point composed
264	% with IsFractionRing.lift). The proof-prose body below (the Hartshorne
265	% I.6.12 chain + scheme-theoretic alternative argument) is unchanged ---
266	% only the input shape changes.
267	\begin{lemma}
268	\leanok
269	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
270	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
271	  
272	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
273	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
274	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
275	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
276	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
277	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
278	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
279	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
280	  % equivalent:}
281	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
282	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
283	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
284	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
285	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
286	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
287	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
288	  (p.~299).}
289	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
290	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
291	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
292	  degree one,
293	  \[
294	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
295	  \]
296	  formally
297	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
298	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
299	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
300	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
301	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
302	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
303	  \[
304	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
305	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
306	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
307	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
308	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
309	  \]
310	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
311	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
312	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
313	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
314	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
315	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
316	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
317	\end{lemma}
318	\begin{proof}
319	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
320	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
321	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
322	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
323	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
324	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
325	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
326	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
327	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
328	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
329	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
330	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
331	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
332	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
333	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
334	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
335	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
336	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
337	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
338	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
339	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
340	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
341	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
342	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
343	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
344	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
345	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
346	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
347	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
348	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
349	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
350	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
351	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
352	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
353	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
354	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
355	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
356	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
357	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
358	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
359	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
360	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
361	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
362	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
363	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
364	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
365	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
366	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
367	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
368	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
369	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
370	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
371	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
372	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
373	\end{proof}
374	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
375	\begin{theorem}
376	
377	\leanok
378	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
379	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
380	  
381	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
382	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
383	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
384	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
385	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
386	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
387	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
388	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
389	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
390	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
391	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
392	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
393	  \[
394	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
395	  \]
396	  Concretely, the Lean signature
397	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
398	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
399	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
400	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
401	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
402	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
403	\end{theorem}
404	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
405	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
406	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
407	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
408	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
409	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
410	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
411	\begin{proof}
412	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
413	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
414	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
415	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
416	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
417	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
418	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
419	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
420	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
421	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
422	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
423	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
424	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
425	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
426	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
427	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
428	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
429	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
430	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
431	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
432	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
433	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
434	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
435	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
436	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
437	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
438	  exists a unique morphism
439	  \[
440	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
441	  \]
442	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
443	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
444	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
445	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
446	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
447	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
448	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
449	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
450	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
451	  \[
452	    \deg(\varphi)
453	    \;=\;
454	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
455	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
456	  \]
457	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
458	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
459	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
460	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
461	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
462	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
463	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
464	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
465	  and
466	  \[
467	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
468	  \]
469	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
470	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
471	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
472	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
473	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
474	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
475	   C.functionField]} instance). By
476	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
477	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
478	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
479	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
480	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
481	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
482	  closing the headline target
483	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
484	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
485	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
486	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
487	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
488	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
489	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
490	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
491	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
492	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
493	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
494	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
495	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
496	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
497	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
498	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
499	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
500	\end{proof}

261	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
262	\label{sec:ab_main}
263	
264	\begin{theorem}
265	
266	\leanok
267	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
268	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
269	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
270	  
271	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
272	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
273	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
274	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
275	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
276	  %  \(\)
277	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
278	  %  \(\)"
279	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
280	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
281	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
282	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
283	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
284	  \[
285	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
286	  \]
287	\end{theorem}
288	
289	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
290	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
291	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
292	%  \(\)
293	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
294	%  \(\)
295	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
296	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
297	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
298	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
299	%  Proposition REF we see that
300	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
301	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
302	%  \begin{align*}
303	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
304	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
305	%  \ldots,\\
306	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
307	%  \end{align*}
308	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
309	%  \begin{align*}
310	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
311	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
312	%  \ldots,\\
313	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
314	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
315	%  \end{align*}
316	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
317	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
318	%
319	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
320	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
321	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
322	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
323	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
324	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
325	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
326	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
327	%  \(\)
328	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
329	%  \(\)
330	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
331	%  shows that
332	%  \(\)
333	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
334	%  M/xM \to 0
335	%  \(\)
336	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
337	%  By Lemma REF we have
338	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
339	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
340	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
341	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
342	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
343	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
344	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
345	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
346	\begin{proof}
347	  
348	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
349	
350	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
351	  finite free resolution
352	  \[
353	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
354	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
355	  \]
356	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
357	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
358	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
359	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
360	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
361	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
362	  sequences
363	  \[
364	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
365	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
366	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
367	  \]
368	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
369	  yields the chain of inequalities
370	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
371	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
372	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
373	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
374	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
375	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
376	
377	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
378	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
379	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
380	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
381	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
382	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
383	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
384	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
385	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
386	  \[
387	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
388	  \]
389	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
390	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
391	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
392	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
393	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
394	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
395	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
396	  \[
397	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
398	  \]
399	  Substituting yields
400	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
401	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
402	\end{proof}

404	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
405	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
406	
407	\begin{definition}
408	
409	\leanok
410	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
411	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
412	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
413	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
414	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
415	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
416	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
417	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
418	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
419	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
420	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
421	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
422	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
423	\end{definition}
424	
425	% NOTE (iter-184 review): the Lean assembly `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a
426	% non-private helper `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal`
427	% (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean L944) that currently carries `:= by sorry` — the
428	% Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap (`IsRegularLocalRing → IsDomain` absent from b80f227).
429	% That helper has no `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter; it is the sole load-bearing
430	% sorry behind `of_regular`. iter-185+ work paths: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in
431	% project (no embedded prime in (0) ⟹ (0) is prime; ~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology
432	% bypass via the now-axiom-clean `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`; (c) Mathlib upstream.
433	\begin{corollary}
434	
435	\leanok
436	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
437	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
438	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
439	  
440	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
441	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
442	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
443	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
444	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
445	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
446	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
447	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
448	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
449	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
450	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
451	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
452	\end{corollary}
453	
454	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
455	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
456	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
457	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
458	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
459	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
460	\begin{proof}
461	  
462	  \leanok
463	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
464	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
465	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
466	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
467	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
468	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
469	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
470	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
471	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
472	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
473	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
474	
475	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
476	  Theorem~REF as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
477	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
478	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
479	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
480	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
481	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
482	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
483	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
484	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
485	\end{proof}
486	
487	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
488	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
489	
490	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
491	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
492	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
493	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
494	
495	\begin{enumerate}
496	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
497	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
498	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
499	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
500	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
501	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
502	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
503	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
504	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
505	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
506	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
507	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
508	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
509	\end{enumerate}
510	
511	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
512	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
513	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
514	Corollary~REF are formalized in
515	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
516	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
517

59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77	
78	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
87	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
88	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
89	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	
97	\leanok
98	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
99	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
101	  
102	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
103	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
104	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
105	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
106	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
107	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
108	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
109	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
110	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
111	
112	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
113	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
114	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
115	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
116	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
117	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
118	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
119	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
120	\end{theorem}
121	
122	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
123	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
124	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
125	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
126	then gives the proof.
127	
128	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
129	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
130	
131	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
132	the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\) at the basepoint \(P_0\). It is the
133	content gated on A.3 (existence of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a representable group
134	scheme).
135	
136	\begin{lemma}\leanok
137	[Abel--Jacobi morphism at a basepoint]
138	  \label{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}
140	  
141	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, the canonical map
142	  % \(f^P\colon C\to J\) (introduction to Proposition~6.1), doc p.~104
143	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
145	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
146	  %  \(f^P\colon C\to J\) has the following universal property: for any map
147	  %  \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending \(P\)
148	  %  to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such that
149	  %  \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
150	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, Summary 6.11, doc p.~107
151	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 113).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
153	  % "SUMMARY 6.11.  Between \((C,P)\) and itself, there is a divisorial
154	  %  correspondence \(\mathcal L^P = \mathcal L(\Delta-\{P\}\times C-C\times\{P\})\).
155	  %    Between \((C,P)\) and \(J\) there is the divisorial correspondence
156	  %  \(\mathcal M^P\); for any divisorial correspondence \(\mathcal L\) between
157	  %  \((C,P)\) and a pointed \(k\)-scheme \((T,t)\), there is a unique morphism
158	  %  of pointed \(k\)-schemes \(\varphi\colon T\to J\) such that
159	  %  \((1\times \varphi)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L\).  In particular,
160	  %  there is a unique map \(f^P\colon C\to J\) such that
161	  %  \((1\times f^P)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L^P\) and \(f(P)=0\)."
162	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1 and
163	  Summary~6.11, \S III.6.}
164	
165	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\), let
166	  \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\). There is a
167	  unique regular morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
168	  \[
169	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J, \qquad
170	    Q \;\longmapsto\; \bigl[\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\bigr],
171	  \]
172	  sending the basepoint \(P_0\) to the identity element \(\eta_J \in J(\bar k)\)
173	  and characterised on \(T\)-points by the formula
174	  \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\), where
175	  $\mathcal L^{P_0} := \mathcal O_{C \times C}\bigl(\Delta - \{P_0\} \times C -
176	  C \times \{P_0\}\bigr)$ is the rigidified diagonal correspondence on
177	  \(C \times C\) and \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) is the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence
178	  on \(C \times J\).
179	\end{lemma}
180	
181	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (no separate verbatim source; the existence of
182	% \(f^P\) is part of Milne's standing setup at the head of \S III.6 -- ``\(J\)
183	% will be its Jacobian variety'' -- and is the moduli-of-degree-0-line-bundles
184	% interpretation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) packaged into Summary~6.11.)
185	\begin{proof}
186	  
187	  By A.3, \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the moduli scheme of degree-zero line bundles
188	  on \(C\); concretely, for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) its \(T\)-points are
189	  isomorphism classes of line bundles \(\mathcal L\) on \(C \times_{\bar k} T\)
190	  whose fibre degree at every geometric point of \(T\) is zero, taken modulo line
191	  bundles pulled back from \(T\) (the universal property of the Picard scheme
192	  packaged in \cref{def:pic_scheme}; the identity component imposes the
193	  degree-zero condition).
194	
195	  Apply this to \(T := C\) and the line bundle
196	  \[
197	    \mathcal L^{P_0} \;:=\; \mathcal O_{C \times_{\bar k} C}\bigl(\Delta -
198	      \{P_0\} \times C - C \times \{P_0\}\bigr),
199	  \]
200	  the rigidified diagonal correspondence on \(C \times_{\bar k} C\). Its fibre
201	  over a \(\bar k\)-point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) along the second projection is the
202	  degree-zero line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\) on \(C\), so \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\)
203	  is a relative degree-zero line bundle, hence a \(C\)-point of \(J\). The
204	  representability of \(J\) at \(T = C\) produces the unique morphism
205	  \[
206	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J
207	  \]
208	  such that \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
209	  for the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) on \(C \times J\).
210	
211	  At the basepoint, restriction of \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\) to \(C \times \{P_0\}\)
212	  gives \(\mathcal O_C(P_0 - P_0) \cong \mathcal O_C\), the trivial line bundle,
213	  whose moduli class in \(J(\bar k)\) is the identity element \(\eta_J\). Hence
214	  \(\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = \eta_J\). Uniqueness of \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the moduli
215	  uniqueness clause of representability: any other morphism \(\iota'_{P_0}\)
216	  satisfying \((1 \times \iota'_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
217	  and the pointed condition agrees with \(\iota_{P_0}\) on \(T\)-points for every
218	  \(T\), hence equals \(\iota_{P_0}\) by the Yoneda embedding.
219	\end{proof}
